data.title: Graphite Miners News For The Month Of March 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the March edition of the graphite miners news.

The past month saw China battery related flake graphite spot prices mixed, but generally flat. Prices remain very depressed.

March saw a really positive month of progress for many of the graphite junior miners.

Graphite price news

During the past 30 days the China graphite flake-194 EXW spot price was flat. The China graphite flake +195 EXW spot price was flat. Note that 94-97% is considered best suited for use in batteries; it is then upgraded to 99.9% purity to make “spherical” graphite used in Li-ion batteries. The spherical graphite 99.95% min EXW China price was up 4.70% the past 30 days. Note: Graphite electrode prices were generally flat.

Note: SMM lists the current China flake-194 graphite spot price at US$346/t.

Graphite demand and supply forecast charts

The IEA Global critical Minerals Outlook 2024 report forecasts graphite demand and supply pipeline to 2040 (source)

Trend Investing v IEA demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

2021 IEA forecast growth in demand for selected minerals from clean energy technologies by scenario, 2040 relative to 2020 - Increases Of Lithium 13x to 42x, Graphite 8x to 25x, Cobalt 6x to 21x, Nickel 7x to 19x, Manganese 3x to 8x, Rare Earths 3x to 7x, And Copper 2x to 3x

Graphite market news

In March Fastmarkets reported: "US duties solidify, prices stagnant post-Lunar New Year."

On March 12 Politico Pro reported:

Feds rebuff tariffs on Chinese graphite. The Commerce Department will cancel steep tariffs it tentatively imposed on graphite imports last month.

On March 16 Investing News Network reported:

ITC ruling blocks proposed tariffs on Chinese graphite...the US International Trade Commission (ITC) ruled that imports from China were not harming the development of a domestic industry...meaning the anti-dumping and countervailing duties identified by Commerce will not take effect...The decision halts duties that had been proposed by the US Department of Commerce earlier this year and removes the threat of tariffs that could have exceeded 160 percent on some Chinese graphite imports.

Note: Bold emphasis by the author.

Graphite miners news

Flake Graphite producers

We have not covered the following Chinese graphite producers but they should also be considered (Ningbo Shanshan Co Ltd (Shanshan Technology) [SHA:600884], BTR New Energy Materials [BJSE:835185], Jiangxi Zichen (private), Kaijin New Energy Technology Corp Ltd (private), Shijiazhuang Shangtai Technology Co. Ltd. [SHE:001301], and Zhongke [SHE:300035]).

Syrah Resources Limited [ASX:SYR][GR:3S7](OTCPK:SYAAF)(OTC:SRHYY)

Syrah Resources Limited owns the Balama graphite mine in Mozambique. Syrah is also a vertically integrated producer of natural graphite Active Anode Material (“AAM”) at their Vidalia facility, Louisiana, USA.

On March 13 Syrah Resources announced:

US ITC reaches final determination on AD/CVD. Syrah Resources Limited (ASX: SYR) (“Syrah” or “Company”) notes that the US International Trade Commission (“ITC”) has reached a final negative determination in the antidumping and countervailing duty (“AD/CVD”) investigation into whether graphite active anode material (“AAM”) imports into the United States from China are materially retarding the establishment of a domestic AAM industry1. With this determination, AD/CVD rates of between 160% and 170%, as determined by US Department of Commerce (“DOC”), will not become effective on AAM imported from China. The final negative determination reverses the preliminary affirmative determination in January 2025, in which ITC determined there was a reasonable indication that Chinese AAM imports were materially retarding the establishment of a US AAM industry...In February2026, DOC determined final aggregate AD/CVD rates of between 160% and 170% on Chinese AAM imports...There remain existing and potential US import tariffs on Chinese natural graphite and synthetic graphite AAM, including tariffs effective under Section 301 and Section 122 of the Trade Act, and tariffs being considered under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act...ITC’s final negative determination on AD/CVD may delay AAM sales from the Vidalia AAM facility and limit near-term demand growth for AAM produced in the United States and Balama natural graphite as feedstock for natural graphite AAM facilities outside China.

Note: Bold emphasis by the author.

On March 26 Syrah Resources announced:

Syrah announces Equity Raising and Strategic Funding Proposals with DFC, DOE and AustralianSuper.

  • Syrah to raise approximately A$104 million (~US$72 million1) through a fully underwritten 1 for 1.32 pro rata accelerated non-renounceable entitlement offer with significant support from AustralianSuper.
  • Non-binding strategic funding proposals received from US International Development Finance Corporation, the US Department of Energy and AustralianSuper to reset Syrah’s balance sheet.

On March 27 Syrah Resources announced:

Syrah successfully completes Institutional Entitlement Offer...The Institutional Entitlement Offer was supported by existing and new institutional shareholders, raising approximately A$44 million (US$30 million1) at a fixed price if A$0.105 per New Share (“Offer Price”). The retail component of the Equity Raising (“Retail Entitlement Offer”), which is fully underwritten, is expected to raise approximately A$61 million (US$42 million1).

Tirupati Graphite [LSE:TGR]

On March 24 Tirupati Graphite announced:

Publication of prospectus, restoration of trading & conversion notice...The Company has requested the restoration of its listing on the Official List and trading on the Main Market of the London Stock Exchange, which has been suspended since August 2024. This is expected to take place later today, 24 March 2026.

Northern Graphite [TSXV:NGC][FRA:ONG] (OTCQX:NGPHF)

Northern Graphite purchased from Imerys the Lac des Iles producing graphite mine in Quebec and the Okanjande graphite deposit/Okorusu processing plant in Namibia. They also own the Bissett Creek graphite project located 100km east of North Bay, Ontario, Canada and close to major roads and infrastructure. The Company has completed an NI 43-101 Bankable final Feasibility Study and received its major environmental permit.

On March 2 Northern Graphite announced: "Northern Graphite partners with Metalshub to digitalize graphite sales."

On March 10 Northern Graphite announced: "Northern Graphite Announces Closing of Flow Through Share Private Placement...at a price of $0.28 per share for gross proceeds of $2,000,000."

On March 12 Northern Graphite announced:

Northern Graphite comments on U.S. Trade ruling on China graphite materials...while it is disappointed with the March 12 decision of the United States International Trade Commission (“ITC”) to not support the findings of the U.S. Department of Commerce (“Commerce”) and impose duties on Chinese graphite materials, the Company believes broader momentum toward establishing a domestic graphite industry in North America remains strong.

NextSource Materials Inc. [TSX:NEXT] [GR:1JW] (NSRCF)

NextSource Materials Inc. 100% owns the Molo Graphite Mine in Madagascar. The Company also has the Green Giant Vanadium Project on the same property.

On March 2 NextSource Materials announced:

NextSource Materials signs agreement for the supply of graphite fines as additional source of feedstock for its Battery Anode Facility in Abu Dhabi...NextSource will source a minimum of approximately 34,000 tonnes and up to 68,000 tonnes of natural graphite fines in total over a seven-year period...These conditions include the commencement of commercial production at NextSource’s BAF in the UAE and successful qualification and final approval of the use of Syrah’s graphite by NextSource and it’s downstream offtake customer.

On March 3 NextSource Materials announced:

NextSource Materials extends Mitsubishi Chemical Offtake Agreement timelines...The extension enables the Company with additional flexibility through July 31, 2027 to satisfy financing, construction, commissioning and first production milestones for its planned Battery Anode Facility (“BAF”) in the United Arab Emirates (“UAE”).

Leading Edge Materials [TSXV:LEM] (LEMIF)

Leading Edge Materials Corp. is a Canadian company focused on becoming a sustainable supplier of a range of critical materials. Leading Edge Materials’ flagship asset is the Woxna Graphite Project and processing plant in central Sweden. The company also owns the Norra Karr REE project, and the 51% of the Bihor Sud Nickel-Cobalt exploration stage project in Romania.

On March 21 Leading Edge Materials announced:

QUARTERLY RESULTS TO JANUARY 31, 2026...During the three months ended January 31, 2026 (“Q1 2026”) the Company reported a net loss of $745,946 compared to a reported net loss of $756,874 for the three months ended October 31, 2025.

Note: There was also some rare earths related news.

Evion Group NL [ASX:EVG]

On March 17 Evion Group announced:

Development and upgrade at PGT continues to meet future demand growth in Europe and the USA.

  • Stage 2 upgrade process has continued as a priority to meet strong demand for expandable graphite from buyers in Europe and the USA.
  • Process required pauses in production throughout March to facilitate installation safely.
  • Sales and exports have continued for the quarter to date.
  • Stage 2 upgrade is important to allow us to achieve production and sales capacity of 4,000 – 4,5000 mtpa and meet substantial demand in the short to medium term.
  • Discussions continue to evolve with external parties on Stage 3 and / or global expansion opportunities.
  • Current actions in the Middle East are expected to cause a global shortage of expandable graphite for 2026 and 2027.

South Star Battery Metals [TSXV:STS] (STSBF) ("South Star")

South Star Battery Metals' Santa Cruz Graphite Mine is in Bahia Brazil. The Company also owns the BamaStar Project in Coosa County, Alabama, USA.

On March 10 Southern Star announced:

Corporate update...Santa Cruz Project Update: As part of the mine restart program, the scrubber/trommel, a critical piece of equipment for materials handling for the Santa Cruz mine, has arrived and been installed at the Santa Cruz mine site. Testing of the scrubber/trommel will begin in the coming week and is expected to take four weeks to commission.

Flake Graphite developers

Renascor Resources [ASX:RNU]

Renascor Resources Ltd. is an Australian exploration company, which focuses on the discovery and development of economically viable deposits containing uranium, gold, copper, and associated minerals. Its projects include graphite, copper, precious metals, and uranium.

On March 16 Renascor Resources announced:

Renascor completes construction of PSG Demonstration Facility...full plant commissioning expected to commence later this month...it has achieved construction completion of its Purified Spherical Graphite (PSG) demonstration facility in Adelaide, South Australia...The demonstration facility is an important component of Renascor’s vertically integrated Battery Anode Material development strategy, incorporating graphite from the Company’s Siviour Graphite Project in South Australia. Once operational, the facility will demonstrate Renascor’s HF-free purification process, positioning Renascor as a globally competitive alternative to China’s PSG supply chain.

Note: There was also some copper/cobalt news.

Talga Group [ASX:TLG] [GR:TGX] (TLGRF)

Talga Group is a technology minerals company enabling stronger, lighter and more functional materials for the multi-billion dollar global coatings, battery, construction and carbon composites markets using graphene and graphite. Talga 100% owned graphite deposits are in Sweden and they have a proprietary process test facility in Germany. Talga plan to build the Luleå battery anode refinery in Sweden as part of their integrated mine-to-anode Vittangi Anode Project.

On March 12 Talga Group announced:

Half Year Financial Report 31 December 2025...During the period, the Group made strong progress across its fully integrated Vittangi Anode Project in Sweden, successfully clearing the majority of remaining regulatory hurdles for the Lulea Anode Refinery and the Nunasvaara South graphite mine...Ended half-year with financial position of A$28.4 million cash plus conditional financing facilities of A$125 million...The Swedish Government formally adopted the Nunasvaara South Graphite Mine Detailed Plan.

Westwater Resources (WWR)

Westwater Resources Inc. is developing the Kellyton Graphite Plant in Alabama, USA. They also own the Coosa Graphite Deposit in Alabama. Details here.

On March 2 Westwater Resources announced:

Westwater Resources files key environmental permit for Coosa Graphite Project. NPDES application submitted to ADEM as part of mine development process.

On March 20 Westwater Resources announced:

Westwater Resources reports Full Year and Fourth Quarter 2025 results.

Key Highlights:

  • Kellyton progress – Continued investment throughout 2025 in the Kellyton Graphite Plant (“Kellyton”) and in the qualification line which is capable of producing more than one metric ton per day of coated spherical purified graphite (“CSPG”) samples to support customer testing and qualification.
  • Coosa Graphite Deposit development – Progressed permitting to support mine development and establish Coosa as a long-term feedstock source for Kellyton.
  • Technology and intellectual property – Received a U.S. patent for the Company’s graphite purification process, supporting domestic production of battery-grade graphite.
  • Kellyton Graphite Plant optimization– Completed additional optimization work on the Phase I development plan, maintaining the estimated capital cost at approximately $245 million despite a rising cost environment.
  • Balance sheet strength – Raised approximately $67 million through convertible notes and equity programs to support project advancement; ended 2025 with $48.6 million in cash with additional capacity remaining under existing financing programs.

Black Rock Mining [ASX:BKT]

NMG completes acquisition of brownfield site in Bécancour to deploy its Active Anode Material production for Panasonic Energy.

  • Clear path to FID with debt commitment representing the first step in financing schedule.
  • Long‑term offtake arrangements underpin project bankability and revenue visibility, with 75% of the Phase-2 Matawinie Mine future production earmarked for the Government of Canada, Panasonic Energy and Traxys.
  • Shovel-ready mine project substantially de‑risked with detailed engineering (~80%), site preparatory work, key permits, and instrumental agreements with First Nation and local community.

On March 26 NMG announced:

NMG Files 2025 Financial Reports and advances Phase 2 Matawinie Mine toward construction...Construction readiness at the Phase 2 Matawinie Mine, including completion of preparatory works, execution planning, significant advancement of detailed engineering, and award of key contracts covering more than 50% of projected CAPEX, within financial estimates.

On March 26 NMG announced:

NMG and Government of Canada execute updated Binding Offtake and Marketing Framework for Matawinie Graphite...Framework covers 30,000 tpa purchase commitment over a seven-year term...Pricing based on a North American fixed price with an annual inflation adjustment.

Greenwing Resources Limited [ASX:GW1] (BSSMF)

Greenwing has graphite projects (Graphmada & Andapa) and a lithium project (Millies Reward) in Madagascar as well as the San Jorge lithium Project in Argentina. Also the Que River polymetallic Project in Tasmania.

Half-Year Report for the period ended 31 December 2025...The Company continues to explore and develop Graphmada for large-scale mining and processing operations along with progressing feasibility studies for the expansion of operations, with a key focus on reducing operating costs and growing production to meet market demand at the lowest possible capital intensity...The Company continues to conduct to care and maintenance activities on site. The Company intends to continue with exploration with a view to increase the Mineral Resource Estimate and provide additional information for future mine planning. Company update...Graphite transaction. Following the execution of a Deed of Settlement on 4 September 2025, the Company has continued to progress the sale of a 70% interest in its Mozambique Graphite Assets to NQM2 Gold, pursuant to binding agreements previously announced. Completion of the transaction remains subject to one outstanding condition precedent, being approval by the Government of Mozambique. The Company understands that this approval process is well advanced, with all other conditions precedent having been satisfied.

EcoGraf delivers US$516m NPV in updated Epanko graphite BFS... outlining a 73,000-tonne-per-annum (tpa) operation with a pre-tax net present value (NPV10) of US$516 million and an internal rate of return of 31.1%... Pre-production capital is estimated at US$181.2 million, with additional resettlement costs of US$18.1 million. Annual EBITDA is projected at US$85.7 million, based on a life-of-mine basket price of US$1,746 per tonne.

On March 16 EcoGraf announced:

EcoGraf Ltd HFfree Technology partnership secures Foxconn battery markets. EcoGraf Limited has announced a significant EcoGraf Ltd HFfree technology partnership with Taiwan's Long Time Technology Co., Ltd. (LTT), a battery materials company with major shareholder Foxconn. The non-binding Memorandum of Understanding positions EcoGraf to expand its HFfree® purification technology into South-East Asian and Taiwanese markets, potentially providing access to Tier-1 OEM supply chains...LTT will evaluate EcoGraf's natural graphite anode products for global ex-China battery facility supply chains.

On March 16 EcoGraf announced: "EcoGraf to partner with Taiwanese battery anode company on development of HFfree Technology."

Lomiko Metals Inc. [TSXV:LMR]

Lomiko has two projects in Canada - La Loutre graphite Project (flagship) (100% interest) and the Bourier lithium Project (70% earn in interest). They also have the Yellow Fox, Antimony, Silver, and Gold Property located in central Newfoundland.

On March 24 Lomiko announced:

Lomiko Metals Inc. delivers Positive Preliminary Feasibility Study for La Loutre Graphite Project...with a pre-tax NPV of CAD$797.5M with 30.3% IRR; after-tax NPV of CAD$617.4M with 24.7% IRR at US$1,524/t Cg ...Initial capital costs (“CAPEX”) of CAD$504.6M. Life of mine processing period (“LOM”) of 28 years.

Metals Australia [ASX:MLS]

NGX Limited (100% owned subsidiary of Sovereign Metals [ASX:SVM] [GR:SVM][LSE:SVML]) (SVMLF)

On March 5 NGX Limited announced:

Half Year Accounts the half year ended 31 December 2025...Malingunde Project in Malawi. NGX continued to assess Malingunde’s development pathway and technical studies...Ongoing engagement and market assessment to explore opportunities for strategic partnerships, future sales and offtake agreements.

Sarytogan Graphite [ASX:SGA]

On March 19 Sarytogan Graphite announced: "$2M cash received from Kazakh Investor Dias Sarsenov."

Focus Graphite [TSXV:FMS][GR:FKC] (FCSMF)

Focus Graphite officially commences government-supported Thermal Purification Project to establish dual-use graphite production in Canada. $14.1M NRCan-funded program begins with six-tonne bulk sample to produce 500 kg high-purity graphite for reactor engineering and product validation.

On March 12 Focus Graphite reported:

Focus Graphite advances Lac Knife infrastructure planning with road and power evaluation; Chairman converts $500,000 loan to equity.

IG6 and Italian chemical group ALKEEMIA to build advanced graphite processing hub in Europe.

  • Operations: ALKEEMIA to manage construction and operate the facility under an Operations and Management Agreement.

  • Production: Initial production target of ~10,000t/y of product from late 2027, with expansion potential.
  • Sales: Advanced negotiations in progress with leading European graphite sales firm.

Albany Graphite Corp. (100% owned subsidiary of Zentek Ltd. [TSXV:ZEN] (ZTEK)

On March 28 Zentek announced:

Triera Biosciences - a Zentek Company - Launches contract research organization services for custom aptamer discovery, biosensor enablement, and machine-learning classification...Triera targets high-priority infectious disease and oncology detection markets across Canada, the United States, and Europe.

[ASX:NXE], Volt Carbon Technologies [TSXV:VCT] (TORVF), Volt Resources [ASX:VRC] [GR:R8L], Reflex Advanced Materials Corp. [CSE:RFLX] (RFLXF), Walkabout Resources Ltd [ASX:WKT].

Conclusion

March saw China battery related flake graphite spot prices mixed, but generally flat. Prices remain very depressed.

Highlights for the month were:

  • The Commerce Department will cancel steep tariffs it tentatively imposed on graphite imports last month.
  • ITC ruling blocks proposed tariffs on Chinese graphite, removes the threat of tariffs that could have exceeded 160% on some Chinese graphite imports. Meaning the anti-dumping and countervailing duties identified by Commerce will not take effect.
  • Syrah Resources to raise approximately A$104 million (~US$72 million1).
  • Tirupati Graphite restores trading on the LSE.
  • Northern Graphite partners with Metalshub to digitalize graphite sales.
  • NextSource Materials signs agreement for the supply of graphite fines (from Syrah) as additional source of feedstock for its BAF in Abu Dhabi.
  • Evion Group - Stage 2 upgrade process has continued as a priority to meet strong demand for expandable graphite from buyers in Europe & USA.
  • Southern Star Santa Cruz Mine restart is progressing.
  • Renascor completes construction of Purified Spherical Graphite Demonstration Facility, full plant commissioning expected later this month.
  • NMG secures senior project debt of US$335m for Phase 2 Matawinie Mine.
  • Falcon Energy Materials initiates US$100 million damages claim against Republic of Guinea.
  • EcoGraf delivers US$516m NPV in updated Epanko graphite BFS, 73,000tpa operation with a pre-tax NPV10% of US$516M and an IRR of 31.1%. EcoGraf Ltd HFfree Technology partnership secures Foxconn battery markets.
  • Lomiko Metals Inc. delivers positive PFS for La Loutre Graphite Project with an after-tax NPV of CAD$617.4M with 24.7% IRR at US$1,524/t Cg.
  • International Graphite ("IG6") and Italian chemical group ALKEEMIA to build advanced graphite processing hub in Europe.

As usual all comments are welcome.

data.title: Navan Q4 Review: Relief Rally Creates Exit Opportunity
data.description:
Shares of Navan, Inc. (NAVN) have been a poor performer since its 2025 IPO, with the stock falling from $25 to ~$8. Navan is a perfect example of a low-profit, high-revenue growth stock using AI to enhance its business, which has come under pressure in recent months as market sentiment has softened. However, on Thursday, shares soared over 40% in a relief rally following better-than-expected quarterly results. Now is a good time to see if this was simply a reaction to oversold conditions or if this rally can continue further.

Navan is disrupting the corporate travel industry

Navan uses an AI-powered platform to help companies’ employees manage corporate travel expenses and make payments all on one platform. This makes it easier for enterprises to manage their overall corporate travel spending, and it also reduces employees’ time in booking their travel, freeing up more of their time to do work on their actual job. In this way, Navan’s value proposition to enterprises is enabling them to both more easily manage their spending and improve employee productivity. It estimates that its offering saves ~15% vs. legacy models with a booking time on average that is less than 10 minutes.

The corporate travel industry is a competitive one. There are large, entrenched players like SAP SE's (SAP) Concur and American Express Company’s (AXP) GBT, among other platforms. Clearly, though, its AI agent approach is working. 90% of bookings are done on the platform, and the average time to book a trip is just 7%. Agent interactions generated the same customer satisfaction score as peers with human agents. This is now generating much better financial results. In the company’s fiscal fourth quarter, Navan earned $0.02, much better than the $0.24 feared loss, as revenue surged 35% to $178 million.

Margin improvement is encouraging

Most impressively, gross margins expanded 400 bps to 72%, driving a ~42% increase in gross profit to $128 million. The company has dramatically improved its margin as it has scaled up over the past two years, going from substantial losses to modest profitability. I would note that its adjusted operating margin excludes share-based compensation (“SBC”); otherwise, the business is still loss-making. Nonetheless, the trajectory is unambiguously positive.

Now, Navan gets 90% of its revenue from commissions paid by suppliers on its platforms (airlines, hotels, etc.), with just 10% tied to subscription revenue paid by enterprise clients. Because it primarily earns revenue from transaction activity, its business is more cyclical than technology companies with a focus on recurring license fees. That does leave growth exposed to any pullback in corporate travel activity. Thus far, the company is seeing limited impact from the Iran conflict, with just low single-digit exposure to the Middle East.

I remain a bit cautious about whether this can persist. While it has limited direct exposure, a prolonged period of elevated inflation could slow growth and lead businesses to reduce discretionary spending activity. In general, curtailing travel is one of the easiest ways for companies to trim spending, and I believe this could be a headwind for growth. The magnitude depends on the duration of the energy market disruption, which is unknowable at this time.

Business mix has not improved

Gross booking value (GBV) jumped 42% to $2.3 billion. For the full year, bookings rose 38%. One thing to note is that booking volumes grew faster than revenue, given pricing compression. This is something that is likely to continue, with management expecting a 30 bp compression in yield during fiscal 2027. Interestingly, bookings rose faster than payment volumes. Over the medium term, NAVN wants to begin capturing payment activity of its customers to earn value across the entire transaction chain and increase recurring revenue. While it is winning more bookings, it is struggling to convert enterprises to its card. That development bears watching.

Separately, in January, Navan announced plans to roll its Reed & Mackay business under its Navan brand. Navan acquired this UK business, which focuses on higher-touch, premium services for executives, in 2021, but it has operated as a standalone brand. While operating one brand makes sense, it will be important during the integration not to disrupt the quality of service that Reed & Mackay customers have come to expect; otherwise, it risks increased attrition. A successful integration should, in my view, open the door to incremental growth by offering customers using its larger Navan platform a premium service option, expanding the potential addressable market.

Share-based compensation is a cost to monitor

Finally, in Q4, the company turned free cash flow positive with ~$30 million of free cash flow. Now, I would note that the company has $47 million of share-based compensation. While a noncash expense, this is a real expense for shareholders given the dilution from the equity issuance. Adjusting for this, free cash flow was still negative. Many technology companies have large share-based expenses, which can inflate their apparent free cash flow, and investors should consider this expense closely in their valuation assessment, in my view.

Navan has a strong balance sheet, which is important as it is still scaling the business to achieve consistently positive free cash flow. It ended the quarter with $616 million of net cash. That gives it plenty of financial flexibility to consider bolt-on M&A or fund further tech development. Eventually, it could use cash to buy back stock and offset the significant dilution from share-based compensation, but I do not view that as likely in calendar year 2026.

Alongside the results, the company offered encouraging guidance. It expects revenue to grow about 30% in Q1 to $205 million, while full-year revenue should be $866-$874 million, or about 24% growth. It is targeting about $60 million of operating income, implying a ~7% margin. I view this outlook as credible, barring a worsening of global travel conditions. While the outcome in Iran is unknown, I fear disruption may be greater for longer, so I expect results to be in the bottom portion of the range, given its reliance on transaction revenue.

This implies about $0.20-$0.23 of operating income/share. However, including share-based compensation, I expect operating income to still be negative in fiscal 2027. In the past few months, we have seen markets punish software companies that have excessive share-based compensation, and valuation screeners that do not account for this expense can make stocks look less expensive than they truly are. In this case, NAVN is likely 18-24 months from being profitable after SBC while also having exposure to travel levels.

Investors should exit NAVN

There also remains the question of whether its use of AI leads to a permanent advantage, or if its competitors, like AXP, that have substantial financial capacity, can develop similar tools. NAVN has proven to be an effective disruptor with ~5% market share, but the durability of its advantage is uncertain and likely to cap valuation, especially with AI tools improving at such a rapid pace, which means that technological advantages can be short-lived.

While the company has improved margins, we are unlikely to see “true” profitability after SBC until fiscal 2029, in my view. Particularly with risks in corporate travel skewed to the downside, I view this rally as an opportunity to exit shares. Their beaten-up nature made them prone to a bounce, which has now happened, creating a better selling point. I view NAVN as a highly speculative stock, and in this market environment, I am cautious about businesses that are not generating underlying profits. As such, I view NAVN as a “sell” and would exit.

data.title: What AI's Threat Might Do To The March 2026 Job Report
data.description:
On April 3, 2026, the Bureau of Labor Statistics will report non-farm payrolls for March. The data might show the impact of the oil crisis on the labor market. The chances are very low that employment will rise like it did in February.

In February, employment trended higher in five main areas: healthcare, financial activities, transportation/warehousing, and social assistance. Jobs in the Federal government fell.

Job Loss Shocker in February

The BLS reported that total nonfarm payroll employment edged down by 92,000 in February. Despite the large figure, the unemployment rate of 4.4% did not change by much. Compared to the year before (in February 2025), the unemployment rate held a range of between 4.0% and 4.2%. Unfortunately, the upcoming March job data might come in weaker for several reasons.

In chart 1 (below, left), the unemployment rate headed toward, but stayed below, 4.5%. Last November, the rate peaked after the BLS did not collect data in October. The downtrend ended in February, when job losses increased.

In chart 2, the BLS posted a dramatic swing of over 100,000 jobs created in January and a drop the month after. Even though the Supreme Court ruled against various Liberation Day 2025 tariffs, a hangover might persist. Higher oil prices compound that problem, affecting trade. Reuters reported that US import prices posted the biggest increase in four years amid a broad rise in goods.

After Iran blocked the Strait of Hormuz in response to the U.S. and Israel's actions, an oil crisis ensued. WTI crude rose from around $65 to around $100. It settled below $91 at the time of writing this article.

The oil prices most affected are those that depend on ships unable to pass the Strait.

The longer the conflict lasts, the greater the impact that higher energy prices will have on the economy beyond a few weeks. Consumers will cut spending as prices rise. Companies will cut jobs to lower their expenses. Technology firms like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), ASML Holding N.V. (ASML), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) will cut staff while committing to growing capital expenditures.

In the establishment data for February, the job decline in professional and business services was 2,000, led by a 9,300 drop in professional, scientific, and technical services. In March, job declines potentially accelerated. Technical consulting services add jobs, softening the decline. However, Gartner, Inc. (IT), Fair Isaac Corporation (FICO), Accenture plc (ACN), and Thomson Reuters Corporation (TRI) are on a sustained year-long downtrend. This weak performance suggests that markets are pricing in AI to replace staff this year.

Increase in Healthcare Jobs

The aging U.S. population will continue to create a high demand for health care jobs. That resulted in a 77,000 job increase in January. In February, health care jobs fell by 37,000. The BLS blamed strike activity for the drop. Despite the broken trend, expect hospitals, health care services, and nursing and residential care facilities to hire staff. AI cannot replace them.

Transportation and warehousing fell by 11,000. Couriers and messengers saw a 17,000 drop in employment. Air transportation added 5,000 to minimize the drop. Again, higher energy costs would raise parcel delivery prices at the U.S. Postal Service, United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS), and FedEx Corporation (FDX). That might pressure firms to cut jobs in March.

Profit margins will face pressures from high energy prices. TFI International Inc. (TFII), Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited (CP), XPO, Inc. (XPO), and Old Dominion Freight Line, Inc. (ODFL) gave up recent gains after the Middle East war broke out. Investors will have a better idea of the headwinds in staff additions in this industry from next week’s jobs report.

Government Job Cuts

A modest drop in government jobs likely continued, especially as severance payments ended. It fell by 10,000 in February and is down by 11% (-330,000) since its peak in October 2024. As I repeatedly wrote in past job preview reports, the departments related to defense and procurement of military equipment will not lose jobs.

The threat from the government to limit bonuses or executive salaries briefly hurt shares of RTX Corporation (RTX), L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX), Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT), and Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC) last fall. Since then, shares have risen to new highs. They peaked on March 3.

Stocks that dipped by more included Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, Inc. (KTOS) and AeroVironment, Inc. (AVAV). Conversely, Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) traded in a range in the last month. Here are the peers to KTOS stock:

Seeking Alpha quant factor grades below warned of the overvaluation in AVAV stock, despite the recent drop.

Both RTX and KTOS have attractive EPS revisions and momentum. Lockheed has a strong profitability grade, while Rocket Lab scored well on growth, momentum, and EPS revisions.

Wage Increase

March’s wage growth needs to keep up with the prior month. In February, the average earnings rose by 0.4%, or 4.8% annualized. The average increase in the past 12 months is 3.8%. The consumer will likely brace for a Consumer Price Index surge in March and for a few months afterward. They need income growth to keep up with inflation. The CPI increased by 0.3% in February and by 2.4% over the last 12 months.

Investors sold consumer discretionary stocks on worries that company profits would worsen. In 2026, The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) gained around 17% before falling back. Compare PG stock to its peers. The SA Quant Rating is a hold for P&G, The Coca-Cola Company (KO), Colgate-Palmolive Company (CL), and The Clorox Company (CLX). Only Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) and Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR), which posted 9% Y/Y revenue growth, have a buy rating.

Coca-Cola stock has a strong set of grades. Although P&G and most of the others score a D- on valuation, investors seeking defense should pick stocks with strong profitability grades.

Your Takeaway

The market will grow increasingly concerned about jobs after the January BLS data. Health care jobs consistently increased monthly, except in February. However, the aging U.S. economy created strong demand for healthcare jobs. AI continues to threaten office jobs where the work is repetitive. The Magnificent 7 companies seeking operating cost cuts to buy AI hardware will cut positions that AI might automate. While automation is nothing new, rapid advances in AI will put pressure on the job-hiring data.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CASH: Reasonable Place To Park Your Cash
data.description:
Introduction

Since its launch in late 2021 Global X High Interest Savings ETF (CASH:CA) has grown into one of Canada's favorite spots to park their cash. It currently has $6.8B in assets, growing from $4.8B two years ago. Its popularity was aided by rising interest rates and yield curve inversion (lower short-term interest rates higher than longer-term rates). Now that Canada has reduced policy interest rates and the yield curve has normalized, I'll compare CASH:CA to other low-risk, fixed income options.

What CASH Holds

Unlike money market funds such as ZMMK:CA which hold short-term treasury bills and commercial paper, CASH:CA holds its assets in high-interest deposit accounts at Canadian chartered banks. Currently, assets are held at the National Bank of Canada (NA:CA), Scotiabank (BNS:CA) and CIBC (CM:CA).

According to CASH's prospectus, it may also invest in "high-quality, short-term (one year or less) debt securities, including treasury bills and promissory notes issued or guaranteed by Canadian governments or their agencies, and banker's acceptances" but its most recent holdings show it does not currently hold any of these investments.

Fees

CASH has a Management Expense Ratio (MER) of 0.11%. This compares favorably to other high-interest savings ETFs such as Purpose High Interest Savings (PSA:CA) and CI High Interest Savings (CSAV:CA) which have MERs of 0.17% and 0.15%, respectively. It's also comparable to money market funds such as ZMMK:CA, which has an MER of 0.13%.

Liquidity

Liquidity is not an issue for CASH. Average daily trading volume over the last 12 months is 1,576,231, with large volumes on the bid and ask and a bid/ask spread of $0.01.

Risk

Unlike when you open a high-interest savings account yourself or invest in Guaranteed Investment Certificates (GIC) your investment in CASH is not covered by the Canada Deposit Insurance Corporation (CIDC) or any other government deposit insurer. This means CASH is subject to counterparty risk at the banks they make their deposits at. The banks that CASH deposits money at are well capitalized and some of the largest in Canada, so the risk is small but important to be aware of.

Current Distribution and Yield

CASH pays monthly distributions with ex-dividend dates near the end of each month, with payments coming approximately a week later. The most recent distribution was February's, which came in at $0.071/ share.

CASH typically trades at $50 a share with regular drops in price each month after the ex-dividend date, followed by climbs as interest accrues over the month.

The most recent distribution works out to an annualized distribution yield of 1.7%, which is a sharp drop (27% decrease) from 2.33% when using the distribution from January 2026. As there hasn't been a drop in Canada's policy interest rate since October 2025 I was surprised by the decrease in distribution. Looking back to February of 2024 something similar, just to a lesser degree, happened as well. The distribution dropped by 10% without any change in Canada's policy interest rate.

Typically the yield of CASH follows closely with that of the Canadian policy rate. To show this, I plotted the annualized yield for CASH and the Canadian Overnight Repo Rate Average (CORRA) each month since Jan 2023. CORRA closely tracks the policy rate but represents actual market transactions. Looking at the chart, it looks like there's a chance that future distributions increase from February's payments of $0.071. If CASH's annualized yield spread compared to CORRA reverted back to what it was over the last 12 months, then CASH's annualized yield would be 2.07%. We'll use both 1.7% and 2.07% when comparing to other short-term fixed-income alternatives.

Comparison of Alternatives

In 2023 and 2024 Canada's yield curve was inverted, and the yield you got from short-term fixed-income investments like CASH was as high or higher than longer-term alternatives. In essence, you weren't getting paid for the duration risk you were taking with longer-duration alternatives. Fast forward to today, and the yield curve has normalized, providing higher yields for longer-duration investments.

The question becomes, how much yield are you willing to sacrifice in order to take no duration risk (buy CASH)? To get an idea of the amount of yield we're leaving on the table, I put together the below comparison of alternatives. CASH has the lowest yield of the options presented (2.07%, assuming the best case that future distributions normalize to the historical CORRA spread). ZMMK, a money market fund, pays a slightly higher yield (2.28%) while still taking on low duration risk (most holdings mature in <180 days) and being highly liquid.

Saven offers a 2.85% yielding savings account, though it's extra work to set up a new account and the interest rate could change at any time.

GICs offer higher yields, with yields increasing out to a year but then leveling off. If you're willing to lock your money up for a year, you can increase your yield by 76% (3.65% vs. 2.07%).

Option Yield Source
CASH:CA 1.7% / 2.07% Annualized Last Distribution / Historical Spread to CORRA
ZMMK:CA 2.28% Annualized Last Distribution
High-Interest Savings Account 2.85% Saven
3 Month GIC 2.60% EQ Bank
6-Month GIC 2.75% EQ Bank
9-Month GIC 2.85% EQ Bank
1-Year GIC 3.65% WealthOne
3-Year GIC 3.70% WealthOne
5-Year GIC 3.85% EQ Bank

Conclusion

CASH is a popular ETF providing a reasonable yield for a highly liquid, zero-duration-risk, fixed-income product. For those willing to sacrifice some liquidity to gain significantly more yield, a 1-year GIC looks like a reasonable choice.

Another alternative is to build a GIC ladder using CASH, 3-month, 6-month, 9-month, and 1-year GICs. This way you'll have capital available for any short-term opportunities and additional influxes of cash coming every 3 months.

data.title: ECB Rate Hikes? Not So Fast
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

There is renewed excitement in financial markets about how the ECB might react to the war in the Middle East, with markets now pricing in more than three rate hikes. Needless to say, in such uncertain times, almost anything is possible, and nothing can be entirely ruled out. The real question, however, is whether markets have looked beyond the headlines and taken on board the messages from Wednesday’s ECB Watchers Conference, including remarks by Philip Lane and Christine Lagarde.

At least in my own humble opinion, those remarks point to a somewhat different reaction function than what markets are now pricing. After last week’s ECB meeting and press conference, it was already obvious that the central bank had made a hawkish pivot and would remain vigilant, arguably more vigilant than in 2022. Back then, however, the ECB was emerging from an extremely accommodative stance and normalising policy from negative interest rates and quantitative easing. With hindsight, the biggest policy mistake was probably the delayed response to an energy price shock that ultimately morphed into a broader inflation surge. Learning from that episode though does not mean a rate hike is imminent.

Lagarde’s speech showed that the ECB, like the rest of us, is currently thinking about a range of different scenarios. As long as the energy price shock remains broadly contained, including first‑round knock‑on effects, it's far from certain that the ECB will react at all. For rate hikes to come back onto the table, the Bank would need to see a rise in inflation expectations and a broadening of inflationary pressures across the economy. So far, the war in the Middle East has instead weighed on business and consumer confidence. Meanwhile, the labour market is entering this energy shock in a weaker position than in 2022, and governments’ fiscal pockets are more constrained, making large‑scale stimulus to offset higher energy prices less likely.

What does this imply for the ECB’s reaction function? While the Bank will continue to update its scenarios, it will be the actual developments, rather than forecasts, that determine the ECB's next steps. The ECB is back to a “driving at sight” approach. Key variables to watch are actual inflation data, survey‑based longer‑term inflation expectations, and wage developments, all of which will be weighed against the risk of slowing economic activity and financial stability concerns.

Looking ahead to the ECB meeting at the end of April, there will be no new forecasts, and only limited data available: March inflation, a handful of country inflation releases for April, and initial estimates of first‑quarter GDP on the day of the meeting. In all honesty, that does not look sufficient to move the needle, unless the ghosts of 2022 are really keeping policymakers awake at night.

For the June meeting, the story is different. Simply put, if we are still talking about the war in the Middle East and high energy prices by then, a rate hike is clearly possible. However, this is not our base case, as we expect the Strait of Hormuz to reopen before then.

In short, our view of the ECB’s reaction function has not changed in recent days. We think the ECB – like us – is expecting an initial inflation wave, starting with gasoline prices, followed by knock-on effects on transportation costs, food prices and other industrial products. As long as this remains a single, time‑limited wave, there is no need for ECB rate hikes. Even though it’s a forbidden word in Frankfurt, some might call this ‘transitory’.

That said, three potential pain points remain for the ECB: a psychological one, ie headline inflation above 4%, reviving uncomfortable memories of 2022; an analytical one, ie core inflation above 3%, signalling broader price pressures; and a credibility one, ie a surge in survey‑based inflation expectations, which would make inaction increasingly difficult to justify.

In today’s environment, almost anything is possible, even ECB rate hikes, but that's still not our base case.

The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Freddie Mac's chief economist says, "The housing market continues to show gradual improvements compared to a year ago amid recent rate volatility.” Sam Khater noted, “Purchase and refinance applications are up year-over-year, and rates remain lower than last year.”

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.38% as of March 26, up from 6.22% last week but below 6.65% in the same period a year ago.

Your next Netflix bill will be higher.

Netflix (NFLX) is raising the price of all its subscription plans by at least a dollar.

The company telegraphed the increase during its earnings call in January.

Netflix says it needs the extra money to help pay for the company’s higher content budget, expected to rise to $20B this year. That’s a $2B increase. This includes a slate of live events, including the World Baseball Classic in Japan, podcasts, and a new license deal with Sony (SONY).

Effective immediately, a standard plan with ads will cost $8.99 a month (+$1), standard with no ads will cost $19.99 a month (+$2), and premium will cost $26.99 a month (+$2). The company also raised its extra member fee by a dollar to $7.99 a month (with ads) and to $9.99 a month (without ads).

Netflix (NFLX) last raised its subscription fees in January 2025.

President Trump said he plans to sign an order to pay Transportation Security Administration (TSA) officers immediately.

This comes at the same time the Senate passed legislation to fund most of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

But that bill still needs approval from the House and President Donald Trump.

The action comes amid a weeks-long funding standoff over DHS which oversees the TSA.

The prolonged partial government shutdown has disrupted airport security leading to staffing shortages and longer wait times at airport checkpoints.

According to a senior administration official, the payments will be funded using money from Trump’s 2025 tax and spending bill.

data.title: GS Tactical Tilt Overlay Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Equity markets sustained their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter of 2025, driven primarily by resilient economic activity, robust corporate earnings from U.S. companies, and continued rate cuts by the Federal Reserve (Fed). Global equities reached new all-time highs despite encountering several headwinds, notably a 43-day U.S. government shutdown that concluded on November 12th and a gradually rising US unemployment rate. While Q3 real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth surged to 4.3% annualized, notably above expectations and supported by 3.5% consumer spending growth, the labor market showed persistent softening, albeit in a linear manner. The unemployment rate climbed from 4.12% in June to 4.56% in November. This labor market moderation, coupled with softer-than-anticipated inflation, prompted the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) to implement 0.25% rate cuts at both its October and December meetings, bringing the federal funds rate to a 3.50%-3.75% range. However, diverging views emerged within the Committee as rates approached neutral levels. In fixed income, the US 10Y Treasury yield remained relatively flat for the quarter, concluding near 4.15% after fluctuating between 3.95% in late October and 4.2% in mid-December as trade tensions eased and growth remained resilient. In equities, global developed markets (unhedged) advanced 3.1%, while emerging markets (unhedged) outperformed with a 4.7% gain, led by Korea's surge driven by AI-related semiconductor demand. Within developed markets, Japanese equities rallied 8.8% following elections and a large fiscal stimulus package announced in November, even as the Bank of Japan raised rates to 0.75%, the highest level since 1995. U.S. large cap equities posted a more modest 2.7% return, with the Russell 2000 gaining 2.2%. Notably, S&P 500 earnings grew 12% year-over-year in Q3, marking the fourth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth.

The Long US Healthcare and Long European Healthcare Equities tilts contributed the most while there were no material tilt detractors in Q4. The healthcare sector is characterized by continuous innovation. In Q4 2025, ongoing advancements in biotechnology, gene therapies, personalized medicine, and medical technology likely continued to fuel investor optimism. Healthcare spending is generally less discretionary than other consumer spending categories. During periods where broader economic growth might be uncertain or slowing, investors often rotate into defensive sectors like healthcare, seeking stability and predictable earnings. This flight to quality may have bolstered healthcare equity performance in Q4 2025.

The first tilt change in October was the addition of a Long Indian Rupee vs Short US Dollar Put Option tilt. The Indian rupee had underperformed its regional peers the most since 2018 and at the time, a trade deal materializing later in the year could help the underperformance reverse course. Next, the US 6m2y Receiver Swaptions structure was removed. The US 2yr yield had fallen approximately 0.30% since the tilt was added in July 2025 and the market had largely priced in the view more quickly than anticipated, leaving the risk-reward of this structure more balanced from current levels until expiry. Finally, the S&P 500 Options tilt position expired at month-end. The position served its intended purpose—preserving participation in a rising market while providing partial downside protection against several key event risks, including the Fed's decision to resume its long-paused rate cutting cycle, U.S. CPI and employment reports, renewed escalation in U.S.-China trade tensions, recent fraudulent credit defaults, and the second-longest U.S. government shutdown in history. With market-friendly outcomes to several of these key risks and the cost of protection higher than when the trade was initiated, the position was not rolled.

There was one tilt removed during November. The COP 5yr Interest Rate Swaps tilt was removed during the early part of the month. Since inception, 5yr rates had risen about 0.50%, reducing the position's value by roughly 1.8%. Inflation expectations exceeded the central bank's target range, driven by wage hikes, and are likely to stay elevated, limiting the potential for rate cuts. Fiscal deficits widened as public spending remained high, likely making further monetary easing improbable. With no immediate positive catalysts and ongoing political and geopolitical risks, the case for lower rates in Colombia was believed to be weak.

There were three tilts removed in December. Both UK Rates tilt positions were removed. At the time of removal, UK rates were pricing in over 2.5 cuts by end-2026, matching ISG's Bank of England forecast. May 2026 local elections may pose political risk, possibly increasing scrutiny of Prime Minister Keir Starmer and creating volatility in Gilt markets, which could present attractive buying opportunities relative to global peers. Overall, valuations were less compelling, and UK political risks had risen. The TOPIX Put Options were allowed to expire in the middle of the month. The index remained well above the strike price, and the position captured the full premium. Earlier in the year, investor concerns about tariffs and politics kept Japanese equity risk premiums high, allowing us to sell out-of-the-money puts for additional yield while retaining potential buying opportunities if markets declined.

As of December 31, 2025. data.title: NOV Inc.: Near-Term Middle East Headwinds Could Become A Tailwind Down The Road - Hold
data.description:
Six months ago, I initiated coverage of NOV Inc. (NOV), a leading provider of equipment and services for the worldwide energy industry, with a “Hold” rating, as the company's solid financial performance was offset by oil price headwinds and resulting weakness in customer demand at that time.

However, things have changed in recent months as escalating geopolitical tensions have sent oil prices soaring, as also shown by the chart of the United States Oil Fund LP ETF (USO) below:

With many of the company's major customers literally printing cash at current oil price levels and the requirement to restore lost production in the Middle East at some point going forward, NOV's medium- and long-term prospects have improved.

However, in the short term, the conflict in the Middle East will likely impact the company's business in the region quite meaningfully. I will provide more color on this topic later in the article.

Last month, NOV reported mixed fourth-quarter results with a revenue beat offset by weaker-than-expected profitability:

Consolidated revenue for the quarter was $2.28 billion, an increase of 5% sequentially and down 1% year-over-year.

Adjusted EBITDA of $267 million was up slightly quarter-over-quarter but down 12% on a year-over-year basis.

On the conference call, management attributed the earnings miss to a combination of unfavorable changes in product mix and higher tariff expense.

However, cash generation continued to be very strong, with free cash flow of $472 million being virtually unchanged from Q4/2024.

During the quarter, the company repurchased 5.7 million shares for $85 million and paid dividends of $27 million.

Earlier this month, NOV increased its quarterly cash dividend by 20% to $0.09 per share.

Please note that the company has committed to returning at least 50% of excess free cash flow to shareholders through a combination of share buybacks and dividends. In addition to the regular quarterly cash dividend, the NOV expects to pay a supplemental cash dividend in Q2.

The company ended the year with $1.55 billion in cash and cash equivalents as well as $1.72 billion in debt. Net debt of just $166 million declined to a new multi-year low.

That said, equipment backlog was down slightly, both on a sequential and year-over-year basis:

Looking ahead, management stated expectations for a challenging 2026 due to weakness in offshore drilling activity, tariffs, and cost inflation.

On the conference call, management projected 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA to decrease slightly from 2025 levels.

In addition, free cash flow is expected to contract quite meaningfully on a year-over-year basis. Based on the projected adjusted EBITDA conversion rate, I would estimate 2026 free cash flow in a range of $400 million to $500 million, down from $876 million in 2025.

Please note that the Middle East was expected to be an exception, with a meaningful number of previously suspended jackup rigs scheduled to go back to work in Saudi Arabia and continued increases in unconventional, land-based drilling activity.

Please note that NOV has a material presence in Saudi Arabia, with Arabian Rig Manufacturing (“ARM”), a 70:30 joint venture with Saudi Aramco (ARMCO), being the flagship asset.

Moreover, the company operates a choke valve and a fiberglass system manufacturing plant in the country. In aggregate, NOV has close to 3,500 employees in the Middle East, approximately 11% of the company's total workforce.

After the recent escalation of the Iran conflict, Saudi Aramco and other national oil companies have partially or completely suspended offshore operations, which is likely to negatively impact orders for offshore equipment for at least the next couple of quarters.

I would expect management to provide more details on the expected impact on the company's operations and financial results on the Q1 conference call in May.

Should the conflict be resolved in due course, the requirement to restore lost oil production should benefit NOV's business in the region quite meaningfully for years to come.

In addition, higher oil prices and resulting strong cash flows for the company's major customers should result in higher capex budgets starting in 2027.

Given the improved medium- and long-term outlook and with industry valuations up across the board, I am raising my assigned EV/EBITDA multiple from 5.5x to 7.0x, which still represents a discount to the broader oil services and equipment industry:

With a higher multiple and valuation now based on estimates for 2027, I am raising my price target from $14.35 to $22.50.

However, given limited upside from current levels and considering near-term uncertainties resulting from the Middle East conflict, I am reiterating my “Hold” rating on NOV's common shares.

Bottom Line

Despite a number of headwinds, NOV has delivered solid results and strong cash flows in recent quarters. The company continues to return substantial amounts of capital to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases.

While 2026 will be impacted by lower offshore drilling activity, higher cost inflation, and particularly the fallout from the war in the Middle East, a resolution of the conflict should benefit NOV quite meaningfully due to the requirement to restore millions of barrels of lost oil production on a daily basis.

The current crisis should also result in efforts to increase oil and gas production in regions outside the Middle East. However, it will take time for new projects to be sanctioned and orders to be placed.

With industry valuations up across the board, I am raising my price target for NOV's shares from $14.35 to $22.50.

However, given limited upside from current levels and considering near-term uncertainties resulting from the Middle East conflict, I am reiterating my “Hold” rating for now.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: What Will Oil Stocks Do If Oil Goes Vertical?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

What will oil stocks do if crude oil really goes vertical? As in, toward its all-time high... or even (gulp) $200 a barrel? Surprisingly, one example from the not-too-distant past suggests energy stock investors might be a little disappointed.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the incredible run in WTI crude oil futures during the infamous 2007-2008 bull market - and compares it to the move in two leading energy ETFs, the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) in black and the State Street SPDR S&P Oil & Gas Exploration & Production ETF (XOP) in blue.

WTI Crude Oil Futures, XLE, and XOP (August 2007-July 2008, % Change)

(Source: TradingView)

You can see that WTI went bonkers - up roughly 100% between August 2007 and July 2008. But XOP rose just 46.2%, while XLE gained only 18.7%. Sure, those gains aren’t bad. But they don’t represent a “double,” either.

How does the 2007-2008 experience compare with what we’ve seen (so far) in 2026? Take a look...

WTI Crude Oil Futures, XLE, and XOP (YTD % Change)

(Source: TradingView)

WTI was up 64% year-to-date as of yesterday afternoon. But once again, XOP was up just 43.9% and XLE was up only 34.9%. That sure beats the 5.2% loss for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). But it shows that when oil prices go ballistic, you won’t necessarily capture it all as an investor in energy stocks.

And it makes sense, when you think about it. If prices really surge in the shorter term, it crushes longer-term demand by kneecapping economic growth.

The economy and markets collapsed in 2008 - and even though that was predominantly because of the mortgage and housing market crisis, $150-a-barrel oil didn’t help. That fear of demand destruction down the road is why energy stock investors won’t pay through the nose upfront.

In this case, sector investors are also worried about damage to Persian Gulf infrastructure and other facilities owned in part by large energy sector players. Plus, they’re concerned about the increased operational costs that energy companies could be stuck with for months or years. That could include higher security, insurance, transportation, personnel, and other expenses.

Bottom line? Yes, higher oil prices are bullish for oil stocks... to a point. But if oil truly goes vertical, your stocks might get left (partially) behind.

data.title: Janus Henderson Short Duration Flexible Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio management

Greg Wilensky, CFA

Michael Keough

Investment environment

  • The U.S. short-term fixed income market delivered strong positive quarterly returns, capping off the sector's best calendar year of performance since 2007. Short-term Treasury yields rallied, helping to drive positive returns and a steeper yield curve. Spread sectors broadly outperformed U.S. Treasuries, with securitized sectors doing particularly well.
  • In December, the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates by 25 basis points (bps) for a third consecutive meeting, bringing policy rates closer to neutral in response to the U.S. unemployment rate hitting a four-year high of 4.6% in November. Other data releases were more positive, with third-quarter gross domestic product (GDP) growth that surprised on the upside.
  • Despite policymakers indicating that they anticipate one more cut in 2026, futures markets are pricing in an additional two to three cuts in 2026. Speculation also continued to build around who would replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell when his term expires in May, and the degree to which their appointment might signal a shift in the central bank's traditional independence.
  • The U.S. 2-year Treasury yield ended the quarter 14 bps lower, at 3.47%. Investment-grade (IG) corporate spreads widened four bps to 78 bps, while high-yield spreads were virtually unchanged at 266 bps, as investor sentiment remained upbeat.

Portfolio review

We strive to generate moderate income from credit spread sectors while limiting drawdowns. We seek to do this by constructing the portfolio with credit risk and a modest amount of interest rate risk over the cycle.

Our overweight allocation to spread risk was the primary contributor to relative performance. Specific contributors included overweight allocations to commercial mortgage-backed (CMBS), agency and non-agency mortgages, high-yield corporates, and loans. Security selection within IG corporates also helped. A portfolio default swap position, which we use to manage the overall level of spread risk in the portfolio, detracted as spread sectors performed well.

We increased our overweight allocation to high yield on the favorable economic outlook and technical backdrop, with proceeds from our reduction in IG corporates. We reduced our allocations to IG corporates due to a less favorable technical backdrop; based on industry data, we expect a growth in supply of 10% or more from the artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure buildout and mergers and acquisitions activity. Our CMBS exposure fell primarily as the result of principal repayments. We also increased our exposure to Treasuries and asset-backed securities (ABS) during the quarter. We maintained our thematic tilts in technology, financials, midstream, and in AI exposure via ABS and CMBS data-center deals.

Our duration, yield curve, and U.S. Treasury futures positioning detracted slightly over the quarter. We remained overweight duration at the front of the yield curve, primarily as a hedge for our overweight allocation to high-quality credit spread sectors. We believe shorter-term Treasury rates would fall further if the economy deteriorated more than expected.

Manager outlook

2025 was a strong year for fixed income, and we are constructive on the outlook for 2026 as well, albeit with a focus on earning carry rather than pursuing significant spread compression.

U.S. economic growth is expected to remain positive, with Fed real GDP projections at over 2.0% for 2026. While some sectors exhibit late-cycle characteristics, resilient consumer spending – supported by tax cuts under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) – and corporate profitability bolstered by AI adoption should sustain economic momentum. Significant AI-driven investments in chips and data centers are anticipated to further fuel growth. AI-driven productivity gains could help reduce inflation, though it also could result in reduced labor demand.

We think the inflationary impact of tariffs on the year-over-year inflation statistics are expected generally to dissipate over the second half of 2026. While the disinflationary progress in energy and housing will continue, overall inflation is likely to remain above the Fed's 2% target through the end of 2026.

Given our positive view on economic growth, we are modestly bearish on interest rate risk, as policy rates and rates further out on the curve might stay higher than expected. That said, we believe the front end of the yield curve will act as a hedge for spread risk if the economy falters, as the Fed has ample room to cut rates.

Key themes within the portfolio include our bullishness on credit spread carry amid robust corporate fundamentals and expected continued economic growth. In addition, the Fed's decision to start growing its balance sheet again should help provide more liquidity, which supports our allocation to fixed income spread sectors. Furthermore, real yields are still above 1% and offer inflation-adjusted return potential. Finally, attractive valuations and strong credit quality within securitized assets relative to corporates support our overweight to securitized sectors.

The currently resilient economy, AI-driven investment, the positive impact of the recent rate cuts, and the possibility of more cuts in the future create a favorable backdrop for fixed income in 2026. Active, diversified strategies focused on carry and risk-adjusted returns remain essential, in our view.
